author: @andrewtaylor940
text: There is a difference between this and Gamergate. Gamergate was mostly an internet slap fight between gamers and journalists. It had virtually no impact on the big game publishers. At least no financial impact. The game makers largely stayed out of it. But this? This impacts the AAA bottom line. A Quarter Million actual money paying PC gamers have joined the group to notify them of which games have been touched by Sweet Baby Stink. That’s $20 million in sales lost, per game title, on day 1. Just on PC alone. Never mind Xbox, PS or Switch. Most games even AAA don’t have enough profit margin to absorb that. This on top of how toxic DEI has become to consumers? s I and similar services are now Radioactive in the board rooms. Guaranteed the developers that went to their Marketing Department and “scared the shit out of them if they don’t hire the extortionist SBI? They will be the first people cut loose in the next layoffs. Because the games cost to much to make now. And the contrast between sales of Spider-Man 2 and Suicide Squad kills the Patriarchy, and Helldivers 2 is not going unnoticed in board rooms.

author: @Katie-hb8iq
text:
In Japan, they produce entertainment and have a clear sense of who the entertainment is for - young boys, young girls, men, adults, women, or specific interests and so on. They don't spend 300 million dollars on 1 project and say they need to make it inclusive for everyone. Capitalism is very much at work. Budgets are smaller, and every type of person can find the things they are into, and every possible genre or interest or age group is essentially served.

We don't have that in the West for some reason. All AAA games must, apparently, cater to everyone, which means they cater to no one. We take brands that cater to men or boys and try to appeal to women when there were not that many women interested in the first place. They also try to appeal to feminists, the LGBT+ community, etc. in these games made for boys and men too.

We should do what the Japanese do, and make development process a lot more efficient and trim down costs appropriate to the target audience. Then, greenlight a lot of products for different audiences rather than trying to spend 300 million dollars on a single AAA project, hoping it caters to everyone (and when it fails, the studio closes). This is why I think the Indie and AA games are doing better these days. They are authentic, and they have a target audience in mind. They are not getting bogged down with external demands. They are just making the game they want to make, and the target audience buys it. I really wish the AAA community would go back to doing the same thing.

Nintendo is doing a lot of things right. I wish more people would copy them. Nintendo shows the world how to make money while most of the rest of the AAA industry is showing the world how to lose money. For the cost of Spider-Man 2, Nintendo can ship a Mario platformer, A Fire Emblem strategy game with dating/friendship elements, a Princess Peach action game aimed at girls, and an Animal Crossing social sim, etc. at a cost almost certainly cheaper than it was to make Spider-Man 2 for the PS5. Some games have All-Age appeal while others definitely have their smaller audience (and none of them have divisive politics). This needs to copied if the industry is to return to health and stability. Nintendo, From Software, and a few others can't be the only healthy AAA studios for a healthy game industry.

In a nutshell, instead of spending 300+ million on a game and trying to manipulate the design and story to appeal to an audience large enough to attempt to make your money back, you should design a game for a specific audience and then budget the project around the expected sales for that audience to ensure you make a profit. Some audiences are naturally going to be larger than others, and thus budgets should be scaled appropriately. But I just don't see how AAA can make games that overly cater to niche groups or members outside of the target audience with such large budgets while turning off the core audience sales numbers - that's the issue. This approach doesn't work and we're not seeing the bulk of successful games follow this high-budget/mass-appeal approach at all. Instead, it's Nintendo games, or things like Palworld or Baldur's Gate 3 or Elden Ring or Helldivers 2.

replies.author: @FordThunderCougarFalconBird

replies.author: @markvargus6519
replies.text:
You are correct in your analysis.  If the major AAA producing companies shrank their expectations and worked to produce games for each niche rather than some all-encompassing AAA title they'd probably be far more financially successful.  However, you ignore the reason that they seek the massive profits on single titles.  They are controlled by boards of directors that are not experienced in games and likely have no connection to any portion of the gamer market or culture.  They were placed because they are financial experts out of Wall Street and they see the problem in a different way.  To them the fewer projects you have going in theory increases the chances of producing one high quality, high customer offering.  It can't work that way mostly because the gamer marketplace is just so segmented and fragmented, but they refuse to understand the culture.

The who addition of politics to games is only another nail in the coffin of the AAA game companies.  Its a clear sign again of the lack of understanding of just how contrary their audience is and how little they are interested in what they keep seeing forced into offerings they find detestable.

replies.author: @mal35m
replies.text: Respectfully I am going to quibble a little with your analysis.  If the big AAA game studios really wanted "everyone"  they would be more like BG3.  The DEI ESG SBI effect is literally to insult the IP and to be openly hostile to the regular gamer base.  These people have posted in public that they don't care if the "wrong" type of gamer does not buy their 70$+ AAA game and surprise surprise they don't.

replies.author: @Katie-hb8iq
replies.text:
@mal35m I disagree. There is a very good case for having lots of diversity in Role-Playing games where character development and player agency are a key part of the gameplay, and being able to accommodate for as many different roles as possible makes sense. 

Not every game is like this, however. Diablo doesn't need feminist and lgbt themes at all. The audience doesn't want that, and it doesn't improve the core gameplay experience. Neither does call of duty and many other types of games. 

It works in BG3 or a Bethesda title. Starfield for example isn't woke. Offering pronouns, player agency, etc. fits what what the player might want to do. For example, you could roleplay a non-binary character while not being non-binary yourself, and the game could respond to this decision in meaningful ways to make that playthrough different.

But I don't see why a Mario platformer, an action rpg or a 1st person shooter needs to incorporate this. It adds nothing at best. And at the worse, it's subtractive and corrective, which is what we've seen from 95% of "diverse" outings in the AAA industry so far and I would say that is undesirable and disrespects the core audience.

If a game is appealing to teenage boys and features attractive women in tight outfits - something comics used to cater to - it doesn't make sense to stop doing this just to appease feminists and puritans on Twitter. This is woke garbage that needs to be injected from these games. It only hurts sales and the franchise at large.

I would argue outside of RPGs, which already had a diverse audience playing them prior to 2018 (for example, they always featured gay characters and relationships, different races, etc.), there isn't much room for a mainstream games targeting boys or men to include these things. Just make other games cater to those audiences.

replies.author: @mal35m
replies.text: @Katie-hb8iq To me there is a difference between offering good looking scantily clad women to attract male gamers who make up most of your customer base or scantily clad muscle men because you want to attract more female gamers or even gay men OR listening to  SBI as it tells companies to purposely "deconstruct" (insult) beloved IPs and to uglify the women because they hate the IP and the customer base.  You are not trying to be inclusive if you purposely disrespect both the IP and your main customer base.  I do concede that some  games like ARPGs like Diablo or the Witcher do not benefit from inclusivity like a RPG like BG3 might if it is a choice and not forced on the player.  I think the key element is giving the player a choice and NOT forcing your narrative on them.

author: @Ichthyodactyl
text:
I can't help but find the irony in a game dev telling people to 'stop falling for it' and to stop 'listening to grifters' as though all of this DEI/ESG stuff isn't exactly that; devs and publishers being sold a false bill of goods by idealogues who are promising some nebulous 'new audience' that has still yet to manifest after more than a decade of such empty promises.

I think that we should, in general as gamers, be vastly more informed about every aspect of the industry that runs our hobby. That includes knowing what the values of the companies are the make the games, publish the games, consult on the games, fund the games, etc. One thing that has been made clear by all of these devs 'coming to the rescue' of SBI is; They do not want to make games that are popular, they want to make games that are 'correct', even if they end up being unpopular (obviously they would prefer both). Being that that's the case and that a lot of these companies can afford to play the game that way because of where their funding is coming from, they have no incentive to prioritize sales (at least short term). They are hoping they can just stay in business long enough for the 'old' playerbase to be replaced by people that are more politically aligned with them. Because of this, we can't afford to just "buy good games and don't buy bad ones", we should still do that obviously, but we also need to be more organized and informed about our purchases in general (and I'm not sure there's really anyone stepping up to the plate to do that to the extent that it would need to be done) and be prepared for the long-haul and actually support good indie studios that are just making good games.

author: @ShelbyT5034
text:
I really don't understand what is going on with this, what I see as nonsense. I am a 55 year old straight white woman who has played video games most of my life. I have played as a male of varied races in numerous narratives (CJ from Grand Theft Auto, Altair from Assassin's Creed, etc...) as well as elves (Link from Zelda) random creatures, monsters and mythical beings. IF and WHEN, I can create my own character, I always lean into some version of "myself" for my own immersion and experience (versus let's say Cloud, Geralt's or Kratos's for example). It has been my experience that diversity has always been present, albeit in a NATURAL organic way (Marcus & Dom- Gears of War). It has only been most recent in my gaming history that I started to notice "sexuality" being a focal point, of which I am not a fan. I have always enjoyed the Call of Duty campaigns, even moreso when I could make a female character for myself. If the story, world development and gameplay appeal to me, it just doesn't matter what the character's race or sex is. I can appreciate the "options" for alternative romance and character creation elements so anyone who plays can find similar immersion for themselves, but I also think there is a point where if those features become the driving force and focus of the game, I've lost interest in it before I even started to play. I do not believe that having thoughts and opinions about DEI theologies in one's consumption of recreation makes them a "BABY", can someone please explain how we have come to this and why? All I know is, if this is the path, I will be dusting off my old systems and grabbing some old game disc (where I won't require internet to access) and continue my gaming experiences retreading my existing library. I am so tired of being told what I am supposed to enjoy, what I can think or say, and what I am supposed to believe.
What is she even talking about, I always experience games from a different perspective and point of view, I'm not a space marine, an ogre, a Jedi or for that matter, a dude. I want off this ride  ;(

author: @53doowopbill

author: @chaz9808

author: @HellsCaretaker
text: Video games used to be a nerd subculture, as was excessive consumption of fantasy or sci-fi movies. That has all changed, now gaming and nerd culture is suddenly mainstream and has become a product that everyone would like to earn money from. And at the same time, every group wants to have a share in what is currently popular. And basically I have no problem with it. If a transsexual wheelchair user with purple hair from Uganda would like to see himself represented in a game or film, no problem - there is something for every taste and you are welcome to offer additional products to make the offer richer. What I reject and don't like at all is when existing franchises and products are subsequently changed or "adapted for a modern audience". Something is artificially forced on EVERYONE else that they don't actually want and that was never part of the product before.

author: @farmdaze9961
text:

author: @Barzarel
text:
Of course people are going to talk about it when every means of escapism is being invaded about that stuff, one the few last places where we can actually take a break from the stupid world nonsense we get bombarded with everywhere. Why would anyone think that would not get serious pushback at one point or another, maybe he doesn't play games, table top games or watch entertainment on TV much. But I am sure whatever escapism it may be implore he would get upset if people came there and inserted themselves into it until it was virtually impossible to enjoy that space of peace and relaxation.

But will give him his probably right about anger engagement farming, people should probably avoid giving them that. Think while spreading awareness definitely help to let people know what to avoid, I think what essentially help more is support alternatives. Like comic industry people been supporting alternatives like Rippaverse, Cyberfrog etc.. and manga comics or what not, for games support indie games that are good and blowing up. This I think work far better because it is eventually going to force industry into pondering why they're not making money while others still are, think that works far better than just boycotting stuff. People did same with Bud light buying from other companies instead. Think that sends a much clearer message than just boycotts.

author: @gameoverinsertcointocontin8102
text: I don't like the term neo-marxist. It doesn't make sense because the original writing was about class and economic divisions, not race. I don't agree with Marx solutions, but he was pretty good at describing the effects of late stage capitalism.
